description: Figure 9 presents quantitative strengthening mechanism analysis for a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel subjected to different hot-rolling reductions (HR45, HR68, and HR82 representing 45%, 68%, and 82% reduction, respectively). Subfigure (a) displays the Hall-Petch relationship plotting the true flow stress at yielding σF0 (obtained by subtracting precipitate strengthening σp and dislocation strengthening σds from yield strength) as a function of the inverse square root of mean effective grain size de^(-1/2). The linear fitting yields a friction stress σ0 of 203.1 MPa and Hall-Petch coefficient of 733.4 MPa·μm^(1/2), with data points showing σF0 increasing from 359.5 MPa (21.82 μm) to 479.3 MPa (7.02 μm) as grain size refines. Subfigure (b) shows stacked bar charts illustrating calculated contributions of individual strengthening mechanisms to yield strength: friction stress (σF, cross-hatched), precipitate shearing (σp-shearing, light red), Orowan bypass (σp-Orowan, red), dislocation strengthening (σds, light blue), and grain boundary strengthening (σgb, dark blue). Grain boundary strengthening (including high-angle grain boundaries and annealing twin boundaries) dominates and increases significantly from HR45 to HR82, while dislocation strengthening also contributes substantially. White triangles indicate experimentally measured yield strengths (σexp), showing good agreement with calculated values and validating that grain boundary and dislocation strengthening are primarily responsible for the strength increment with increasing hot-rolling reduction.
